question: An application embeds the Zeebe client library to connect to the engine. Which two responsibilities does the docs say the application can carry out via that client?

options.text: Actively call Zeebe (start process instances, correlate messages, deploy definitions) and subscribe to jobs created from BPMN service tasks.
Only start process instances; subscribing to service-task jobs requires calling the REST API directly without the client.
Only subscribe to jobs; starting instances must be triggered from Operate or Tasklist.
Drive the broker over JDBC and push events into Zeebe via a JMS bridge.

a.text: The docs say the application can do two things with the client: actively call Zeebe (e.g., start process instances, correlate messages, deploy process definitions) and subscribe to tasks created in the engine for BPMN service tasks.

b.text: The client itself handles both starting instances and subscribing to jobs; there is no need to drop down to raw REST for subscriptions.

c.text: Starting process instances via the client is one of the two named responsibilities — it is not restricted to Operate or Tasklist.

d.text: The docs describe gRPC or REST as the underlying remote protocols hidden by the client, not JDBC or JMS.

explanation: The Zeebe client library is the application's two-way handle on the engine: it lets you actively call Zeebe (start instances, correlate messages, deploy definitions) and subscribe to jobs that BPMN service tasks create. Both halves are needed for a typical glue-code service.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world
